message.text: Inline policy userpolicy is attached directly to an IAM user. Policies should be attached to groups; direct user attachment is hard to audit.
IAM access key resource user is declared in IaC. Confirm there is a rotation process (manual/automated) — CIS requires rotation every 90 days.

RDS instance default: storage is not encrypted at rest. Database snapshots and backups are stored in cleartext.
RDS instance default is flagged publicly_accessible = true. A public DNS endpoint is attached; any IP can reach the port.
RDS instance default: automated backups are disabled (backup_retention_period = 0). Point-in-time recovery is unavailable.
RDS instance default has multi_az = false. A single-AZ failure will cause an outage for the duration of cross-AZ recovery (typically 30-90 minutes).
RDS instance default: deletion_protection is disabled. An accidental terraform destroy will permanently delete the database.

Aurora cluster app1-rds-cluster: IAM database authentication is disabled. Applications must use long-lived username/password credentials instead of short-lived IAM tokens — credentials must be rotated manually.

RDS instance default: monitoring_interval = 0. Enhanced Monitoring (OS-level CPU, memory, disk, network per process) is disabled. Standard CloudWatch metrics expose only aggregate DB metrics — not the per-process visibility needed to diagnose query-induced CPU spikes or memory pressure.
EC2 instance should have EBS root volume encryption enabled: cannot verify — required attribute(s) missing from source: root_block_device.encrypted
EC2 instance should have EBS root volume encryption enabled: cannot verify — required attribute(s) missing from source: root_block_device.encrypted
EC2 instance db_app: no metadata_options block declared. AWS defaults to http_tokens = "optional" (IMDSv1 enabled). SSRF-based credential theft via the instance metadata service remains possible.
EC2 instance web_host: no metadata_options block declared. AWS defaults to http_tokens = "optional" (IMDSv1 enabled). SSRF-based credential theft via the instance metadata service remains possible.
EBS volume web_host_storage: encryption is not enabled. Data at rest on the volume is stored in cleartext — detached volumes or decommissioned hardware expose raw block data.
Lambda function analysis_lambda uses runtime nodejs12.x, which is deprecated per AWS policy. No further security patches are issued for this runtime.
Lambda function should have tracing (X-Ray) enabled: cannot verify — required attribute(s) missing from source: tracing_config.mode
Lambda function analysis_lambda: no dead_letter_config. Async-invocation failures retry twice and disappear silently — no signal, no audit trail.
Lambda function analysis_lambda: code_signing_config_arn is not set. Any artifact pushed to the function passes — there's no signing-publisher gate.

Lambda function analysis_lambda: no explicit aws_cloudwatch_log_group declared. Lambda will auto-create one with default retention (never expires) and AES256 encryption — no CMK, no controlled retention.
KMS key logs_key: automatic rotation is disabled. Long-lived key material increases cryptographic compromise blast radius.
KMS key logs_key: deletion_window_in_days is less than the maximum of 30. If scheduled for deletion, there is less time to cancel and recover data encrypted with this key.
ECR repository repository: image tags are mutable. The same tag (e.g., `latest`, `v1.2.3`) can point at different image SHAs over time — supply-chain attacks become invisible.

ECR repository repository: no aws_ecr_lifecycle_policy references this repository. Old image versions accumulate forever — at $0.10/GB/month, hot CI repos can grow to $200-1000/month before anyone notices.
EKS cluster eks_cluster: no encryption_config block. Kubernetes Secrets are stored unencrypted in etcd — anyone with etcd-level access (or backup access) reads them in plaintext.
EKS cluster runs an end-of-life Kubernetes version: cannot verify — required attribute(s) missing from source: version
VPC eks_vpc: no aws_flow_log resource references this VPC. Network-level forensics (lateral movement, exfiltration) are unreliable post-incident.
Security group permits 0.0.0.0/0 on a known debug or management port: cannot verify — required attribute(s) missing from source: ingress.cidr_blocks

Subnet eks_subnet1: map_public_ip_on_launch = true. Every instance launched into this subnet receives a public IP automatically — including future instances created by ASGs, ECS, or EKS without an explicit private-only override.

Subnet eks_subnet2: map_public_ip_on_launch = true. Every instance launched into this subnet receives a public IP automatically — including future instances created by ASGs, ECS, or EKS without an explicit private-only override.

Subnet web_subnet: map_public_ip_on_launch = true. Every instance launched into this subnet receives a public IP automatically — including future instances created by ASGs, ECS, or EKS without an explicit private-only override.

Subnet web_subnet2: map_public_ip_on_launch = true. Every instance launched into this subnet receives a public IP automatically — including future instances created by ASGs, ECS, or EKS without an explicit private-only override.

RDS default: apply_immediately = true. Any terraform apply that touches this instance's parameter_group, instance_class, engine_version, etc. will trigger an immediate reboot or replacement. On a busy production DB this is a self-inflicted outage waiting to happen.

Lambda analysis_lambda: no aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm references this function. Errors land in CloudWatch but nobody is paged. The next time this Lambda starts throwing 500s, the on-call learns about it from a customer ticket.

Lambda analysis_lambda: no alarm on Throttles. When this function gets throttled (own cap or account cap), invocations fail silently from the caller's perspective — neither logs nor metrics-as-configured surface it as a paging signal.

RDS default: no alarms reference this instance. CPU saturation and connection-pool exhaustion are the two failure modes engineers don't catch until customers complain. Both have CloudWatch metrics; neither has a configured threshold here.

Lambda analysis_lambda: X-Ray tracing is Active but no aws_xray_sampling_rule references this function. At default sampling, high-volume functions produce more trace data than is useful AND bill more than expected.

Lambda analysis_lambda: this plan declares no aws_cloudwatch_dashboard. If incidents are debugged by typing CloudWatch URLs into the AWS console one tab at a time, the next on-call engineer will spend their first 5 minutes rebuilding mental state someone else already had.

EKS cluster eks_cluster: <= 1 aws_eks_node_group references this cluster. Single-pool clusters can't drain workloads across pools during a kernel upgrade or instance-family migration.
OpenSearch monitoring-framework: cluster_config.instance_count = 1. Any maintenance event takes the cluster down for the duration; instance replacement loses unreplicated shards.
RDS default: max_allocated_storage unset. The instance can't grow beyond its allocated_storage value. A surprise write spike → ENOSPC → forced downtime to bump storage manually.
Aurora cluster app1-rds-cluster: backup_retention_period = 0. One-day retention means a Friday-night incident discovered Monday morning is past the PITR window.
Aurora cluster app2-rds-cluster: backup_retention_period = 1. One-day retention means a Friday-night incident discovered Monday morning is past the PITR window.

aws_iam_user user declared. Each IAM user is a long-lived credential surface. Legitimate cases: external system integrations that don't support IRSA / OIDC federation. Humans should go through Identity Center; pods should use IRSA; CI/CD should use OIDC.

aws_iam_access_key for user aws_iam_user.user.name: a long-lived AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID / AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY pair is being managed. These end up in .env files, screenshots, Stack Overflow, and Git history.
IAM policy ec2policy: combines Resource: "*" with sensitive action prefixes. The blast radius of a token compromise is the entire account for those service classes.
RDS storage_type = gp3 but iops explicitly set (likely unnecessary): cannot verify — required attribute(s) missing from source: storage_type
Lambda function is configured with maximum memory (3008 MB+): cannot verify — required attribute(s) missing from source: memory_size

VPC eks_vpc: no aws_vpc_endpoint declared. Secrets Manager calls from in-VPC workloads traverse NAT. At ~$0.045/GB through NAT, even modest secret-fetching patterns pay back the endpoint cost in days.

VPC web_vpc: no aws_vpc_endpoint declared. Secrets Manager calls from in-VPC workloads traverse NAT. At ~$0.045/GB through NAT, even modest secret-fetching patterns pay back the endpoint cost in days.
VPC eks_vpc: no aws_vpc_endpoint for KMS. Decrypt calls leave the VPC via NAT — for high-volume workloads this adds up faster than the endpoint cost.
VPC web_vpc: no aws_vpc_endpoint for KMS. Decrypt calls leave the VPC via NAT — for high-volume workloads this adds up faster than the endpoint cost.
Cluster eks_cluster: no aws_vpc_endpoint declared for ECR or S3. Container image pulls + Docker layer downloads flow through NAT — common single-biggest contributor to NAT data-processing charges.
eks_cluster: no aws_inspector2_enabler in the plan. Container / runtime / EC2 CVEs aren't surfaced — the team is doing vulnerability management manually or not at all.
repository: no aws_inspector2_enabler in the plan. Container / runtime / EC2 CVEs aren't surfaced — the team is doing vulnerability management manually or not at all.
No aws_inspector2_enabler declared (no vulnerability scanning): cannot verify — required attribute(s) missing from source: name
Load balancer weblb: access logging is not enabled. Request-level forensics (source IP, path, status, latency, backend) are unavailable after an incident — you cannot reconstruct who hit what.
Classic ELB weblb: no HTTPS/SSL listener — every listener is plaintext HTTP/TCP. Traffic between clients and the load balancer, including session tokens, crosses the wire unencrypted.

Neptune cluster default: storage_encrypted is not enabled. The graph — often identity, permission, and relationship data — is stored unencrypted. Encryption cannot be turned on in place; it requires a snapshot-restore rebuild.
Neptune cluster default: audit logging is off (enable_cloudwatch_logs_exports has no "audit"). Gremlin/SPARQL queries are never recorded — after a credential compromise you cannot tell what was read.
Neptune cluster default: IAM database authentication is disabled. Access depends on static credentials instead of short-lived IAM tokens, so a single leaked secret has a much larger blast radius.
OpenSearch domain monitoring-framework: at-rest encryption is disabled. Index data, snapshots, and logs are stored in cleartext on the underlying EBS volumes.
OpenSearch domain monitoring-framework: HTTPS is not enforced. Queries and documents are transmitted in cleartext — credentials and data are interceptable.
OpenSearch domain monitoring-framework: node-to-node encryption is disabled. Intra-cluster shard replication traffic is unencrypted — interceptable within the VPC.

OpenSearch domain monitoring-framework: fine-grained access control (FGAC) is disabled. All authenticated requests have full read/write access to every index — no per-index or per-document authorization.

IAM policy userpolicy: enables privilege escalation — deploy code with elevated Lambda role — granted on an unconstrained resource (Resource: "*") with no Condition. Any principal holding this policy can escalate to broader or administrator privileges.

IAM policy ec2policy: grants data exfiltration actions (s3:GetObject, rds:DownloadDBLogFilePortion) on Resource: "*" with no Condition. Any principal holding this policy can bulk-read or export data across all accessible resources of that service — without needing to escalate privileges first.

IAM policy userpolicy: grants data exfiltration actions (s3:GetObject) on Resource: "*" with no Condition. Any principal holding this policy can bulk-read or export data across all accessible resources of that service — without needing to escalate privileges first.

IAM policy ec2policy: grants resource-exposure actions (s3:PutBucketPolicy, s3:PutBucketAcl, s3:PutObjectAcl) on Resource: "*" with no Condition. Any principal holding this policy can modify the access policy of S3 buckets, Lambda functions, SQS queues, KMS keys, or other resources — potentially making them publicly accessible.

IAM policy userpolicy: grants resource-exposure actions (s3:PutBucketPolicy, s3:PutBucketAcl, s3:PutObjectAcl, lambda:AddPermission, lambda:AddLayerVersionPermission) on Resource: "*" with no Condition. Any principal holding this policy can modify the access policy of S3 buckets, Lambda functions, SQS queues, KMS keys, or other resources — potentially making them publicly accessible.

Toxic combination on role ec2role: it is assumable by a compute service (EC2 / ECS / Lambda), AND its attached policies grant data exfiltration actions (s3:GetObject, rds:DownloadDBLogFilePortion) on Resource: "*" with no Condition. An attacker who compromises that workload (SSRF, RCE, dependency takeover) can steal the instance/task role credentials and obtain those permissions. An attacker who assumes this role can bulk-exfiltrate account data without any further privilege escalation.

IAM policy ec2policy: grants infrastructure-modification actions (ec2:RunInstances, ec2:RequestSpotInstances, rds:CreateDBInstance, rds:RestoreDBInstanceFromDBSnapshot, ec2:AuthorizeSecurityGroupIngress, ec2:CreateVpc, ec2:CreateKeyPair) on Resource: "*" with no Condition. Any principal holding this policy can launch EC2 instances, run ECS tasks, or modify network rules without restriction — a common cryptomining and lateral-movement vector.

IAM policy userpolicy: grants infrastructure-modification actions (ec2:RunInstances, ec2:RequestSpotInstances, ec2:AuthorizeSecurityGroupIngress, ec2:CreateVpc, ec2:CreateKeyPair) on Resource: "*" with no Condition. Any principal holding this policy can launch EC2 instances, run ECS tasks, or modify network rules without restriction — a common cryptomining and lateral-movement vector.

Toxic combination on role ec2role: it is assumable by a compute service (EC2 / ECS / Lambda), AND its attached policies grant infrastructure-modification actions (ec2:RunInstances, ec2:RequestSpotInstances, rds:CreateDBInstance, rds:RestoreDBInstanceFromDBSnapshot, ec2:AuthorizeSecurityGroupIngress, ec2:CreateVpc, ec2:CreateKeyPair) on Resource: "*" with no Condition. An attacker who compromises that workload (SSRF, RCE, dependency takeover) can steal the instance/task role credentials and obtain those permissions. An attacker who assumes this role can launch arbitrary compute for cryptomining or lateral movement — no privilege escalation needed.

Toxic combination on role ec2role: it is assumable by a compute service (EC2 / ECS / Lambda), AND its attached policies grant resource-exposure actions (s3:PutBucketPolicy, s3:PutBucketAcl, s3:PutObjectAcl) on Resource: "*" with no Condition. An attacker who compromises that workload (SSRF, RCE, dependency takeover) can steal the instance/task role credentials and obtain those permissions. An attacker who assumes this role can make S3 buckets, KMS keys, Lambda functions, or SQS queues publicly accessible — creating a data-exfiltration path without directly reading data.

Lambda function `analysis_lambda` exposes `secret_key` as a plaintext environment variable. The value is readable by anyone with lambda:GetFunctionConfiguration — including the AWS Console, CloudTrail, and any CI/CD role with describe access.

Lambda function `analysis_lambda` exposes `access_key` as a plaintext environment variable. The value is readable by anyone with lambda:GetFunctionConfiguration — including the AWS Console, CloudTrail, and any CI/CD role with describe access.

Elasticsearch domain `monitoring-framework` has no vpc_options (public endpoint) and advanced_security_options.enabled is not true. Without fine-grained access control, anonymous HTTP requests can reach the cluster's REST API — enabling full index read/write, cluster configuration, and snapshot exfiltration from any internet host.
Variable "company_name": no `type` declaration and no validation block. Mistakes surface as cryptic provider errors instead of clear plan-time messages.
Variable "environment": no `type` declaration and no validation block. Mistakes surface as cryptic provider errors instead of clear plan-time messages.
Variable "profile": no `type` declaration and no validation block. Mistakes surface as cryptic provider errors instead of clear plan-time messages.
Variable "region": no `type` declaration and no validation block. Mistakes surface as cryptic provider errors instead of clear plan-time messages.
AWS provider: no default_tags block. Every resource has to tag itself for Cost Explorer — teams forget on a third of them.
AWS provider: no default_tags block. Every resource has to tag itself for Cost Explorer — teams forget on a third of them.
corpus/terragoat/terraform/aws/providers.tf: S3 backend — dynamodb_table missing. State files often contain secrets; concurrent applies without a lock corrupt state.
Variable "environment": no `type` declaration and no validation block. Mistakes surface as cryptic provider errors instead of clear plan-time messages.
Variable "environment": no `type` declaration and no validation block. Mistakes surface as cryptic provider errors instead of clear plan-time messages.

Plan includes hourly-billed resources (1 aws_db_instance, 1 aws_eks_cluster, 2× aws_instance, 9× aws_rds_cluster) but no aws_budgets_budget. Estimated monthly spend from priceable resources in this plan: ~$13/month (us-east-1 on-demand). Without a cost alarm, a forgotten instance, misconfigured autoscaling, or credential compromise can run up an unbounded bill before the team notices.

S3 buckets (flowbucket, data, financials, operations, data_science, logs) have no aws_s3_bucket_lifecycle_configuration. Without lifecycle rules, objects accumulate forever: old versions from versioning, abandoned multipart uploads, and stale data all bill at $0.023/GB-month with no bound.
Security group is declared but no resource attaches it. Likely a leftover from a deleted service.
IAM role ec2role: permissions_boundary unset. Future policy attachments can grant unlimited privilege — the role's effective permissions = union of all attached policies, no ceiling.
IAM role iam_for_eks: permissions_boundary unset. Future policy attachments can grant unlimited privilege — the role's effective permissions = union of all attached policies, no ceiling.
IAM role iam_for_lambda: permissions_boundary unset. Future policy attachments can grant unlimited privilege — the role's effective permissions = union of all attached policies, no ceiling.

shortDescription.text: IAM policy enables privilege escalation

fullDescription.text: IAM policy ec2policy: grants data exfiltration actions (s3:GetObject, rds:DownloadDBLogFilePortion) on Resource: "*" with no Condition. Any principal holding this policy can bulk-read or export data across all accessible resources of that service — without needing to escalate privileges first.

shortDescription.text: IAM policy grants bulk data exfiltration capability

fullDescription.text: IAM policy ec2policy: grants resource-exposure actions (s3:PutBucketPolicy, s3:PutBucketAcl, s3:PutObjectAcl) on Resource: "*" with no Condition. Any principal holding this policy can modify the access policy of S3 buckets, Lambda functions, SQS queues, KMS keys, or other resources — potentially making them publicly accessible.

shortDescription.text: IAM policy grants resource permission modification capability

fullDescription.text: Toxic combination on role ec2role: it is assumable by a compute service (EC2 / ECS / Lambda), AND its attached policies grant data exfiltration actions (s3:GetObject, rds:DownloadDBLogFilePortion) on Resource: "*" with no Condition. An attacker who compromises that workload (SSRF, RCE, dependency takeover) can steal the instance/task role credentials and obtain those permissions. An attacker who assumes this role can bulk-exfiltrate account data without any further privilege escalation.

shortDescription.text: Broadly-assumable IAM role with data exfiltration capability

fullDescription.text: IAM policy ec2policy: grants infrastructure-modification actions (ec2:RunInstances, ec2:RequestSpotInstances, rds:CreateDBInstance, rds:RestoreDBInstanceFromDBSnapshot, ec2:AuthorizeSecurityGroupIngress, ec2:CreateVpc, ec2:CreateKeyPair) on Resource: "*" with no Condition. Any principal holding this policy can launch EC2 instances, run ECS tasks, or modify network rules without restriction — a common cryptomining and lateral-movement vector.

shortDescription.text: IAM policy grants unscoped infrastructure launch/modification capability

fullDescription.text: Toxic combination on role ec2role: it is assumable by a compute service (EC2 / ECS / Lambda), AND its attached policies grant infrastructure-modification actions (ec2:RunInstances, ec2:RequestSpotInstances, rds:CreateDBInstance, rds:RestoreDBInstanceFromDBSnapshot, ec2:AuthorizeSecurityGroupIngress, ec2:CreateVpc, ec2:CreateKeyPair) on Resource: "*" with no Condition. An attacker who compromises that workload (SSRF, RCE, dependency takeover) can steal the instance/task role credentials and obtain those permissions. An attacker who assumes this role can launch arbitrary compute for cryptomining or lateral movement — no privilege escalation needed.

shortDescription.text: Broadly-assumable IAM role with infrastructure modification capability

fullDescription.text: Toxic combination on role ec2role: it is assumable by a compute service (EC2 / ECS / Lambda), AND its attached policies grant resource-exposure actions (s3:PutBucketPolicy, s3:PutBucketAcl, s3:PutObjectAcl) on Resource: "*" with no Condition. An attacker who compromises that workload (SSRF, RCE, dependency takeover) can steal the instance/task role credentials and obtain those permissions. An attacker who assumes this role can make S3 buckets, KMS keys, Lambda functions, or SQS queues publicly accessible — creating a data-exfiltration path without directly reading data.

shortDescription.text: Broadly-assumable IAM role with resource permission modification capability

fullDescription.text: KMS key logs_key: automatic rotation is disabled. Long-lived key material increases cryptographic compromise blast radius.

shortDescription.text: KMS customer-managed key should have rotation enabled

fullDescription.text: KMS key logs_key: deletion_window_in_days is less than the maximum of 30. If scheduled for deletion, there is less time to cancel and recover data encrypted with this key.

shortDescription.text: KMS key deletion window should be the maximum (30 days)

fullDescription.text: Lambda function analysis_lambda uses runtime nodejs12.x, which is deprecated per AWS policy. No further security patches are issued for this runtime.

shortDescription.text: Lambda function uses an end-of-life runtime

fullDescription.text: Lambda function should have tracing (X-Ray) enabled: cannot verify — required attribute(s) missing from source: tracing_config.mode

shortDescription.text: [Needs Review] Lambda function should have tracing (X-Ray) enabled

fullDescription.text: Lambda function analysis_lambda: no dead_letter_config. Async-invocation failures retry twice and disappear silently — no signal, no audit trail.

shortDescription.text: Lambda function has no dead-letter queue configured

fullDescription.text: Lambda function analysis_lambda: code_signing_config_arn is not set. Any artifact pushed to the function passes — there's no signing-publisher gate.

shortDescription.text: Lambda function does not enforce code signing

fullDescription.text: Lambda function analysis_lambda: no explicit aws_cloudwatch_log_group declared. Lambda will auto-create one with default retention (never expires) and AES256 encryption — no CMK, no controlled retention.

shortDescription.text: Lambda function has no explicit CloudWatch log group

fullDescription.text: Lambda function `analysis_lambda` exposes `secret_key` as a plaintext environment variable. The value is readable by anyone with lambda:GetFunctionConfiguration — including the AWS Console, CloudTrail, and any CI/CD role with describe access.

shortDescription.text: Lambda function has a plaintext secret in environment variables

fullDescription.text: Neptune cluster default: storage_encrypted is not enabled. The graph — often identity, permission, and relationship data — is stored unencrypted. Encryption cannot be turned on in place; it requires a snapshot-restore rebuild.

shortDescription.text: Neptune cluster is not encrypted at rest

fullDescription.text: Neptune cluster default: audit logging is off (enable_cloudwatch_logs_exports has no "audit"). Gremlin/SPARQL queries are never recorded — after a credential compromise you cannot tell what was read.

shortDescription.text: Neptune cluster has no audit logging

fullDescription.text: Neptune cluster default: IAM database authentication is disabled. Access depends on static credentials instead of short-lived IAM tokens, so a single leaked secret has a much larger blast radius.

shortDescription.text: Neptune cluster does not enforce IAM database authentication

fullDescription.text: OpenSearch domain monitoring-framework: at-rest encryption is disabled. Index data, snapshots, and logs are stored in cleartext on the underlying EBS volumes.

shortDescription.text: OpenSearch domain does not encrypt data at rest

fullDescription.text: OpenSearch domain monitoring-framework: HTTPS is not enforced. Queries and documents are transmitted in cleartext — credentials and data are interceptable.

shortDescription.text: OpenSearch domain does not enforce HTTPS

fullDescription.text: OpenSearch domain monitoring-framework: node-to-node encryption is disabled. Intra-cluster shard replication traffic is unencrypted — interceptable within the VPC.

shortDescription.text: OpenSearch domain does not encrypt node-to-node traffic

fullDescription.text: OpenSearch domain monitoring-framework: fine-grained access control (FGAC) is disabled. All authenticated requests have full read/write access to every index — no per-index or per-document authorization.

shortDescription.text: OpenSearch domain has fine-grained access control disabled

fullDescription.text: Long-running Lambda has no DLQ and no reserved concurrency: cannot verify — required attribute(s) missing from source: timeout

shortDescription.text: [Needs Review] Long-running Lambda has no DLQ and no reserved concurrency

fullDescription.text: Lambda integration timeout shape (timeout config sanity): cannot verify — required attribute(s) missing from source: timeout

shortDescription.text: [Needs Review] Lambda integration timeout shape (timeout config sanity)

fullDescription.text: Long-running Lambda with low memory — likely workload misfit: cannot verify — required attribute(s) missing from source: timeout

shortDescription.text: [Needs Review] Long-running Lambda with low memory — likely workload misfit

fullDescription.text: RDS default: apply_immediately = true. Any terraform apply that touches this instance's parameter_group, instance_class, engine_version, etc. will trigger an immediate reboot or replacement. On a busy production DB this is a self-inflicted outage waiting to happen.

shortDescription.text: RDS apply_immediately = true (destructive changes outside maintenance window)

fullDescription.text: Lambda analysis_lambda: no aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm references this function. Errors land in CloudWatch but nobody is paged. The next time this Lambda starts throwing 500s, the on-call learns about it from a customer ticket.

shortDescription.text: Lambda function has no CloudWatch alarm on Errors metric

fullDescription.text: Lambda analysis_lambda: no alarm on Throttles. When this function gets throttled (own cap or account cap), invocations fail silently from the caller's perspective — neither logs nor metrics-as-configured surface it as a paging signal.

shortDescription.text: Lambda function has no CloudWatch alarm on Throttles metric

fullDescription.text: RDS default: no alarms reference this instance. CPU saturation and connection-pool exhaustion are the two failure modes engineers don't catch until customers complain. Both have CloudWatch metrics; neither has a configured threshold here.

shortDescription.text: RDS instance has no CloudWatch alarm on CPU or DatabaseConnections

fullDescription.text: Lambda analysis_lambda: X-Ray tracing is Active but no aws_xray_sampling_rule references this function. At default sampling, high-volume functions produce more trace data than is useful AND bill more than expected.

shortDescription.text: Lambda has X-Ray tracing Active but no custom sampling rule

fullDescription.text: Lambda analysis_lambda: this plan declares no aws_cloudwatch_dashboard. If incidents are debugged by typing CloudWatch URLs into the AWS console one tab at a time, the next on-call engineer will spend their first 5 minutes rebuilding mental state someone else already had.

shortDescription.text: Lambda exists but no CloudWatch dashboard declared in the same plan

fullDescription.text: RDS engine_version pinned to major version only: cannot verify — required attribute(s) missing from source: engine_version

shortDescription.text: [Needs Review] RDS engine_version pinned to major version only

fullDescription.text: EKS cluster eks_cluster: <= 1 aws_eks_node_group references this cluster. Single-pool clusters can't drain workloads across pools during a kernel upgrade or instance-family migration.

shortDescription.text: EKS cluster declares a single node group

fullDescription.text: OpenSearch monitoring-framework: cluster_config.instance_count = 1. Any maintenance event takes the cluster down for the duration; instance replacement loses unreplicated shards.

shortDescription.text: OpenSearch / Elasticsearch domain runs on a single instance

fullDescription.text: RDS default: max_allocated_storage unset. The instance can't grow beyond its allocated_storage value. A surprise write spike → ENOSPC → forced downtime to bump storage manually.

shortDescription.text: RDS instance has no max_allocated_storage (no storage autoscaling)

fullDescription.text: Aurora cluster app1-rds-cluster: backup_retention_period = 0. One-day retention means a Friday-night incident discovered Monday morning is past the PITR window.

shortDescription.text: Aurora cluster has backup_retention_period = 1 (default minimum)

fullDescription.text: IAM role ec2role: permissions_boundary unset. Future policy attachments can grant unlimited privilege — the role's effective permissions = union of all attached policies, no ceiling.

shortDescription.text: IAM role has no permissions_boundary

fullDescription.text: aws_iam_user user declared. Each IAM user is a long-lived credential surface. Legitimate cases: external system integrations that don't support IRSA / OIDC federation. Humans should go through Identity Center; pods should use IRSA; CI/CD should use OIDC.

shortDescription.text: IAM user resource declared (use Identity Center / SSO instead)

fullDescription.text: aws_iam_access_key for user aws_iam_user.user.name: a long-lived AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID / AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY pair is being managed. These end up in .env files, screenshots, Stack Overflow, and Git history.

shortDescription.text: IAM access key resource declared (long-lived credential)

fullDescription.text: IAM policy ec2policy: combines Resource: "*" with sensitive action prefixes. The blast radius of a token compromise is the entire account for those service classes.

shortDescription.text: IAM policy with privileged action + Resource: "*"

fullDescription.text: RDS storage_type = gp3 but iops explicitly set (likely unnecessary): cannot verify — required attribute(s) missing from source: storage_type

shortDescription.text: [Needs Review] RDS storage_type = gp3 but iops explicitly set (likely unnecessary)

fullDescription.text: Lambda function is configured with maximum memory (3008 MB+): cannot verify — required attribute(s) missing from source: memory_size

shortDescription.text: [Needs Review] Lambda function is configured with maximum memory (3008 MB+)

fullDescription.text: default: no lifecycle.prevent_destroy block. A terraform destroy or refactor that removes this resource address will delete it without a guardrail.

shortDescription.text: Stateful resource missing lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true }

fullDescription.text: AWS provider: no default_tags block. Every resource has to tag itself for Cost Explorer — teams forget on a third of them.

shortDescription.text: AWS provider block has no default_tags

fullDescription.text: corpus/terragoat/terraform/aws/providers.tf: S3 backend — dynamodb_table missing. State files often contain secrets; concurrent applies without a lock corrupt state.

shortDescription.text: S3 backend without encrypt = true or DynamoDB lock

fullDescription.text: Variable "company_name": no `type` declaration and no validation block. Mistakes surface as cryptic provider errors instead of clear plan-time messages.

shortDescription.text: Variable declared without type or validation

fullDescription.text: VPC eks_vpc: no aws_vpc_endpoint declared. Secrets Manager calls from in-VPC workloads traverse NAT. At ~$0.045/GB through NAT, even modest secret-fetching patterns pay back the endpoint cost in days.

shortDescription.text: VPC has no Secrets Manager VPC endpoint (Lambda → Secrets traffic via NAT)

fullDescription.text: VPC eks_vpc: no aws_vpc_endpoint for KMS. Decrypt calls leave the VPC via NAT — for high-volume workloads this adds up faster than the endpoint cost.

shortDescription.text: VPC has no KMS VPC endpoint

fullDescription.text: Cluster eks_cluster: no aws_vpc_endpoint declared for ECR or S3. Container image pulls + Docker layer downloads flow through NAT — common single-biggest contributor to NAT data-processing charges.

shortDescription.text: EKS / ECS cluster present but no ECR VPC endpoints

fullDescription.text: eks_cluster: no aws_inspector2_enabler in the plan. Container / runtime / EC2 CVEs aren't surfaced — the team is doing vulnerability management manually or not at all.

shortDescription.text: No aws_inspector2_enabler declared (no vulnerability scanning)

fullDescription.text: RDS instance default: storage is not encrypted at rest. Database snapshots and backups are stored in cleartext.

shortDescription.text: RDS instance storage should be encrypted at rest

fullDescription.text: RDS instance default is flagged publicly_accessible = true. A public DNS endpoint is attached; any IP can reach the port.

shortDescription.text: RDS instance should not be publicly accessible

fullDescription.text: RDS instance default: automated backups are disabled (backup_retention_period = 0). Point-in-time recovery is unavailable.

shortDescription.text: RDS instance should have automated backups enabled (backup_retention_period > 0)

fullDescription.text: RDS instance default has multi_az = false. A single-AZ failure will cause an outage for the duration of cross-AZ recovery (typically 30-90 minutes).

shortDescription.text: RDS production instance should have Multi-AZ enabled

fullDescription.text: RDS instance default: deletion_protection is disabled. An accidental terraform destroy will permanently delete the database.

shortDescription.text: RDS deletion protection should be enabled

fullDescription.text: Aurora cluster app1-rds-cluster: storage encryption is disabled. All data files, redo logs, backups, and automated snapshots are stored in cleartext.

shortDescription.text: Aurora cluster does not encrypt storage

fullDescription.text: Aurora cluster app1-rds-cluster: deletion protection is off. The cluster (and all data) can be deleted with a single API call — including by a confused-deputy attack via an over-privileged role.

shortDescription.text: Aurora cluster has deletion protection disabled

fullDescription.text: Aurora cluster app1-rds-cluster: backup retention is 0 — automated backups are disabled. Data loss from an incident, corruption, or accidental deletion cannot be recovered via point-in-time restore.

shortDescription.text: Aurora cluster backup retention period is 0 (no automated backups)

fullDescription.text: Aurora cluster app1-rds-cluster: IAM database authentication is disabled. Applications must use long-lived username/password credentials instead of short-lived IAM tokens — credentials must be rotated manually.

shortDescription.text: Aurora cluster does not enable IAM database authentication

fullDescription.text: RDS instance default: monitoring_interval = 0. Enhanced Monitoring (OS-level CPU, memory, disk, network per process) is disabled. Standard CloudWatch metrics expose only aggregate DB metrics — not the per-process visibility needed to diagnose query-induced CPU spikes or memory pressure.

shortDescription.text: RDS instance has Enhanced Monitoring disabled (monitoring_interval = 0)

fullDescription.text: S3 bucket flowbucket: no aws_s3_bucket_public_access_block references this bucket. Public access posture is one mis-applied policy or ACL away.

shortDescription.text: S3 bucket has no Block Public Access companion resource

fullDescription.text: VPC eks_vpc: no aws_flow_log resource references this VPC. Network-level forensics (lateral movement, exfiltration) are unreliable post-incident.

shortDescription.text: VPC has no associated flow logs

fullDescription.text: Security group permits 0.0.0.0/0 on a known debug or management port: cannot verify — required attribute(s) missing from source: ingress.cidr_blocks

shortDescription.text: [Needs Review] Security group permits 0.0.0.0/0 on a known debug or management port

fullDescription.text: Subnet eks_subnet1: map_public_ip_on_launch = true. Every instance launched into this subnet receives a public IP automatically — including future instances created by ASGs, ECS, or EKS without an explicit private-only override.

shortDescription.text: VPC subnet automatically assigns public IPs on launch

fullDescription.text: Security group is declared but no resource attaches it. Likely a leftover from a deleted service.

shortDescription.text: Security group web-node has no consumers
